course_description: With the economies of China and India growing rapidly, Asia is emerging as one of the key drivers of global economic growth. The purpose of this course is to understand the environmental and organisational issues confronted by firms doing business in Asia. These include various aspects of the external environment such as social, economic, political, cultural, legal, customer and competitive factors, as well as the strategic and organisational responses of firms to the complex, diverse and dynamic business challenges in Asia. Using real-life company cases, participants will learn how to take managerial decisions and solve business problems confronted by firms in Asia.

requirements.incompatible: BA877 or BF877 or MGMT7402 or BSEB3304 or 7312 or IBUS3304
requirements.assumed_background: Initiative to learn, think, apply and share knowledge about doing business in Asia.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims:
Countries in Asia are often seen by Australian exporters as being different from each other but essentially the same within. In reality, there are great differences between regions within each country. China and Japan are classic examples. Each country has been affected by its geography, religions, politics and history. Each has its own approach to business. To understand how business is done, it is important to understand both the written rules (laws) and unwritten rules ( business conduct and behaviour). Understanding the written rules is easy. Undestanding the unwritten rules requiures study and insight.

To capture the diversity of the Asian region, we will study key social, cultural, economic, geographic, legal ,political and historical characteristics of selected countries in South -East and North -East Asia namely, China, Taiwan, Japan, South Korea, Thailand, Vietnam, Laos, Cambodia, Indonesia, Malaysia,the Philippines, East Timor, Myanmar ( Burma), Brunei and Singapore. We will study the similarities and differences among these countries, as well as between Australia and the countries in Asia. To do business in Asia, managers need to develop a deep understanding of the contextual factors in each country and design and implement international business strategies that fit the socio-economic-political context of the country.

The objective of country analysis is to understand the diverse business contexts in these countries, including the macro-environment such as social, economic, political, cultural and legal factors, and the micro-environment such as customer and competitive characteristics. Another objective is to learn how firms do business in the Asian region- how the game is played. We will examine a number of company cases covering a wide range of problem situations. Case studies help us understand how country differences impact on business strategy and managerial decision-making in different countries. We will also explore a numbler of international business concepts and strategies that are unique to Asia.

Students doing this course will learn some of the unique characteristics that typify the Asian region, and the challenges confronted by firms operating in Asia - one of the most dynamic regions in today's global economy. Overall, the course will benefit participants who wish to learn more about Asia and how to do business in the region.

learning_outcomes.description: Think independently, critically and creatively, and
Behave ethically and with social responsibility.

assessment_details.title: In-class presentation 5%, Written Report 10%
assessment_details.task_description:
Each student must present a 10-15 minute paper to the class on one of the following topics to be selected for the student by the lecturer, and at a time scheduled by the lecturer, and hand in at the end of the presentation a 1000 word written version for assessment :

1) Discuss the religion and colonial history of the country ( for that week) and its effect on daily life and doing business.

2) Discuss the overt and covert links between government and business in that country and what an outsider ( eg an Australian firm) needs to know to do business successfully in that country .

Students will be assessed on both the presentation and the written paper. The written paper must be soundly researched and referenced. A full reference list must be provided. Referencing will not count in the word limit.

assessment_details.title: written paper
assessment_details.task_description:
.Students must write and submit a 2000 word written paper on a topic of choice from the list below:

1) SE and NE Asian economies are experiencing difficult economic times - but there are always opportunities for Australian business. Choose a country from those being studied in this course and identify a business opportunity in that country still remaining for Australian firms today. Give reasons for your answer. Your paper must be fully referenced and your arguement based on a sound analysis of all the relevant business environments, backed by recent and good quality published information.

2) You are the manager of an Australian firm that manufactures windmills for electrical power generation. These range in size from small generators for individual households to larger units for towns and industry. By analysis, set out which country in the NE or SE Asian region ( among those studied in the course) that you think offers the greatest opportunity as an export market for Australian windmill generators. Set out your arguments based on the political, legal, geographic and other environments that support your choice. Your paper must be fully referenced and your findings must be based on recent and good quality published research information.

Note: The reference list and referencing is not included in the word count. The due date for the paper is the Wednesday of the last week of lectures for the semester, handed in at the student resource centre. A late penalty will apply according to faculty guidelines.

learning_resources.required_resources:
There is no required text for the course, as there is nothing commercially available that covers all the areas well. However, students are referred to copies of a text held in the SS and H library for reference. This is Burns, R. ( latest edition) Doing Business in Asia. Longman Press, Melbourne.

There is a huge amount of information on the web that can be easily accessed through Google for any topic in each of the countries of interest. Most country governments in Asia have websites for access and most western countries trading with Asia have information for readily available and free for access by exporters (eg Japan, China, Australia ( DFAT) , New Zealand, Canada, the USA - especially the CIA world factbook. For each country students need to know something of its history, culture, geography, law and religions. This gives a better understanding of how and why business is done in particular ways.
learning_resources.recommended_resources: Please refer to other learning resources.

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
A large number of references are available in the library on the topics covered in the course, including books, academic journals, business magazines and the internet. Students are encouraged to actively use these sources to prepare for the lectures, and to do research for their country and case analyses projects. The following is a brief list of useful references for the course.

BOOKS ON ASIA

Burns, Robert. 1998. Doing Business in Asia: A Cultural Perspective . South Melbourne: Longman.

Chen, Ming-Jer. 2001. Inside Chinese Business: A Guide for Managers Worldwide . Boston, MA: Harvard Business School Press.

Dunung, Sanjyot P. 1998. Doing Business in Asia: The Complete Guide. San Francisco, CA: Jossey-Bass.

El Kahal, Sonia. 2001. Business in Asia Pacific: Text and Cases . Oxford: Oxford University Press.

Lasserre, Philippe and Schutte, Hellmut. 1999. Strategies for Asia Pacific: Beyond the Crisis . New York: Palgrave.

Schneider, Susan C. and Barsoux, Jean-Louis. 2003. Managing across Cultures . Essex: Prentice Hall.

Toews, Bea and McGregor, Robert. 1998. Succeed in Business: The Essential Guide for Business and Investment in Thailand . Singapore: Times Editions.

Yip, George S. 2000. Asian Advantage: Key Strategies for Winning in the Asia-Pacific Region. Perseus Books, Cambridge, MA.

BOOKS ON INTERNATIONAL BUSINESS

Badaracco, J.L. 1991. The Knowledge Link: How Firms Compete through Strategic Alliances . Boston: Harvard Business School Press.

Bartlett, C.A. & Ghoshal, S. 1989. Managing Across Borders: The Transnational Solution . Boston: Harvard Business School.

Bartlett, C.A. Ghoshal, S. & Birkinshaw, J.M. 2004. Transnational Management: Text, Cases and Readings . Boston: McGraw-Hill.

Czinkota, M.R. Ronkainen, I.A. & Moffett, M.H. 2004. Fundamentals of International Business. Mason, Ohio : Thomson.

Douglas, S.P. & Craig, C.S. 1983. International Marketing Research . Engelwood Cliffs, N.J.: Prentice-Hall.

Dunning, J.H. (Ed.).1998. Globalisaiton, Trade and Foreign Direct Investment . Amsterdam: Elsevier Science.

Geringer, M.J. 1988. Joint Venture Partner Selection- Strategies for Developed Countries . 1e. New York: Quorum.

Hofstede, Geert. 2001. Culture's consequences: Comparing Values, Behaviors, Institutions, and Organizations across Nations. Thousand Oaks, London: Sage.

Howard, Robert. (Ed.). 1993. The Learning Imperative: Managing People for Continuous Innovation . Boston: Harvard Business School.

Jain, Subhash C. 1996. International Marketing Management . 5e. Ohio: South Western College.

Porter, Michael E. (Ed.). 1986. Competition in Global Industries . Boston: Harvard Business School.

Prahalad, C.K. & Doz, Y. 1987. The Multinational Mission: Balancing Local Demand and Global Vision . New York: Free Press.

Root, F.R. 1994. Entry Strategies for International Markets . New York: Lexington.

Schneider, Susan C. and Barsoux, Jean-Louis. 2003. Managing across Cultures . Essex: Prentice Hall.

Terpstra, V. and Sarathy, R. 2000. International Marketing . 8e. Fort Worth: Dryden.

Yoshino, M.Y. and Rangan, U.S. 1995. Strategic Alliances: An Entrepreneurial Approach to Globalisation . Boston: Harvard Business School.

Laos, Thailand, Philippines introduction (Lecture): Country analyses for Laos, Thailand, first part of Philippines analysis. Student presentations on Laos and Thailand

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the Universitys assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity
It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism
The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Academic Integrity and Plagiarism policy ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25128 ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Feedback on Assessment
Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25109 )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment
Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment is not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers. Please follow the procedure below:

Students visit the UQ Business School Reception area, and complete a Viewing of Examination form.

The original form is kept by the School Office and a copy is given to the student.

Completed forms must be submitted to the School Office by the end of the first week of the subsequent semester at the latest.

Examination papers of those students who have submitted a completed form to the School Office will be available for viewing during the following relevant session.

The sessions are scheduled as follows: in the case of first semester examinations during the third teaching week in semester two; and in the case of second semester examinations and summer examinations during the third teaching week in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands (see below).

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation. Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The location and the hours during which papers may be viewed will be published on the School's website and will be available from the School Reception area.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results
Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the course co-ordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the course co-ordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Director of Studies of the BEL Faculty.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses
Students on a placement course also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25120 ) for detailed information.

Working with Children
Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25004 ) to find out how to apply for a blue card.

Students with a Disability
Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Adviser at Student Support Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25122 ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25111

Where an adjustment is made to an accredited program, it is the responsibility of the relevant Faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of the change/s.

Occupational Health and Safety
Undergraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25055 ) and Postgraduate Students ( http://www.uq.edu.au/hupp/index.html?page=25057 ) should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory.

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

COMPUTING FACILITIES
Courses offered by the School of Business extensively utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia, or Building 8 at Ipswich, or lower level of Gatton library for further details.

UQ BUSINESS SCHOOL EXCELLENCE IN TEACHING AWARDS
The University of Queensland business School's "Award for Teaching Excellence" seeks to recognize and promote excellent teaching practices within the School. In any given calendar year there can be up to two awards presented (one for instructors of postgraduate courses, and one for instructors of undergraduate courses).

To nominate a lecturer you need to write a letter or send an email to the details below. Your nomination must clearly indicate the category (undergraduate or postgraduate) for which the nominee is to be considered and also your reasons for their nomination.

Nominations must reach the head of School by 4p.m. Friday 18 September 2009. Please address nominations to:

Award for Teaching Excellence Nominations
Head of School
c/- UQ Business School
University of Queensland Qld 4072

Or via email to e.skipper@business.uq.edu.au using Award for Teaching Excellence Nominations in the subject space.

Further information can be obtained by contacting the Head of School's Personal Assistant ( e.skipper@business.uq.edu.au ) or The Chair of Teaching and Learning (Fiona Rohde) via f.rohde@business.uq.edu.au .

course_introduction:
Before attempting this course, students are advised that it is important to complete the appropriate prerequisite course(s) listed on the front of this course profile. No responsibility will be accepted by the School of Business, the Faculty of Business, Economics and Law or the University of Queensland for poor student performance occurring in courses where the appropriate prerequisite(s) has/have not been completed, for any reason whatsoever.

It is the student's responsibility to attend classes and consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including examination times. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Postgrad Coursework) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A2 . A broad understanding of the field of study, including how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A3 . A comprehensive and in-depth knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A7 . An appreciation of the link between theory and practice .
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
B5 . The ability to practise as part of an interdisciplinary team .
C2 . The ability to work and learn independently and effectively .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C5 . The ability to formulate and investigate problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
C6 . The abilities and skills that provide a foundation for future leadership roles.
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D4 . The ability to process material and to critically analyse and integrate information from a wide range of sources.
D5 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions using an evidence-based approach .
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E3 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .
E7 . The ability to work effectively and sensitively across all areas of society .
E8 . An understanding of and respect for the roles and expertise of associated disciplines .

other_learning_activities_information:
Consultation
Teaching staff are there to assist you. Staff members have set consultation times during which you may see them without making an appointment. Consultation times for staff are posted on the course website and on individual staff member's doors. Consultation outside of these times is by appointment only.
If you have questions or points that require clarification, you may visit during consultation times. If staff members are unavailable, leave a message with your name and telephone number or send them an email.
Where applicable, you may also use the course's online Discussions to seek assistance from both your peers and staff. Messages will be posted on this medium regularly and you must take the time to read these each week. All content related discussions (ie announcements about the course) will take place in the Main topic that is a public forum that everyone in the course is able to access and read.

Administration
All content related questions and specific questions re the administration of this course should, in the first instance be directed to the tutor/s involved in delivery of the course. Content matters that cannot be dealt with by the tutor should then be referred to the course co-ordinator. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School Office on Level 2 Colin Clark Building, St Lucia Campus or Level 3 Building 12, Ipswich Campus.

course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made in writing (not via email) to the Course Coordinator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application together with the Course Coordinator's and the student's signatures and a copy returned to the student.

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% (0.4 of a grade) of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue.

The above late submission information does not apply to honours theses, 8 unit research theses, 4 unit research projects and 2 unit research reports. For extension and late submission information in relation to these courses see section 5.4 of the course profile.
other_assessment_information:
Students are expected to attend all lectures and come to the class well prepared with the lecture topics and questions for discussion. The class will be divided into seven groups. From weeks 4 to 10, one group will present their case analysis each week. Each group will get maximum 20 minutes for the case presentation . In addition to the presentation, the groups will submit a written report about their respective case analyses on the day of the presentation or latest by 5pm the next day. Group assignments on country analyses are scheduled for presentation in weeks 11 and 12. For the country presentation, each group will get maximum 3 0 minutes for the presentation. The written report on country analyses is due by 5pm Wed 15th October 2008.

In addition, during weeks 3 to 10, each group will submit two written analyses of maximum one-page each of the country and the case scheduled for discussion in the class. Both reports should be type written. The country report should briefly describe a recent/current news/event related to the country, and briefly discuss the implications of the news/event for doing business in the country. The news/event may be about the country environment or about a company doing business in the country. Similarly, the case report should contain a brief analysis of the case based on the guidelines provided in the case project marking guide. The country and the case refer to the topics in the weekly schedule. Both reports must be submitted at the beginning of the class each week.

Every member of the team should participate in the presentation, and be fully familiar with the group report that is submitted and presented in the class. The instructor and the participants can ask any member of the presenting team for clarifications on their report and presentation. After the presentation, each group in the class will complete a peer assessment of the presentation, and ask at least one good question to the presenting group for discussion and/or clarification. Initially, a single grade will be awarded to all members of the team. A written peer review should be submitted alongwith each of your written assignments. Individual grades will be reduced in proportion to the individuals contribution to the group work as indicated in the peer review reports. It is essential that you work well in teams and make equal contribution to the group tasks.

Detailed marking guide for country and case reports and presentations will be given in the class and posted on the course website. Additional assessment criteria for the reports and presentations are as follows:

Answering all parts of the assignment questions

Relevance, importance and comprehensiveness of submissions

Adequate length (balance between short and inadequate answers versus long and irrelevant answers)

Illustrations with appropriate figures and examples

Continuity and linkage across sections and topics

Quality of presentation (critical and analytical rather than descriptive; addressing the 'how', 'why' and 'so what' questions in addition to the 'what' question)

Linking empirical observations with appropriate theoretical/conceptual frameworks

Overall organization, clarity, completeness, consistency, conceptualization, expression, style and editing (e.g., spell-check, paragraph formatting, layout, page numbers, citations and references, numbering and referencing of tables, figures and diagrams)

Overall, the report must be well integrated, with a single underlying theme linking all parts of the report. Include key figures, tables and data in the main report, and secondary information and analysis in the appendix. Include only relevant information in the tables and discuss all tables in the text. Information in tables that is not discussed in the text of the report will be disregarded for assessment purposes. Please do not simply "cut and paste" large amount of information - text and tables - from secondary sources. All information should be analysed and evaluated critically for the purpose of getting credit in the assessment. Since the report and the presentation are evaluated separately and independently, both should contain the information required to address the assessment criteria. All assignments and reports must be typed in 12-font, 1.5 lines spacing and 2.5 cms margins on all sides. PLEASE SUBMIT BOTH HARD AND SOFT COPIES OF THE REPORTS BY THE DUE DATE. Email the soft copy to the lecturer in a single, virus free file. Use the following format for your filename, "class day and time-group number-company name", for example, "Wed10-A1-Telstra". Include the same filename in the subject line of your email and provide details of your group members in the body of the email such as name, student number and percent contribution to group work.

ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

St Lucia students: (Please read the section above for additional guidelines about assignment submission.) Unless otherwise specified all assignments are to be submitted through the BEL Faculty Resource Centre (FRC) located on level 1 of the Colin Clark Building. Each assignment must be accompanied by the appropriate, fully completed FRC Assignment Cover Sheet, available from www.bel.uq.edu.au or the FRC. Assignments must not be faxed or emailed to the Course Coordinator without prior arrangement. All assignments will be stamped with the date of receipt, or if submitted after hours with the date of the next business day. Students must sign the cover sheet, acknowledging the plagiarism Statute of the University and must always keep a copy of their work. All group assignments must have ONE nominated representatives details at the top of the coversheet, with details of other group members listed on the lower section. Students should keep a list of their group members' full names and contact details. Note that students must produce their student ID card (or drivers license) to collect marked assignments.

Ipswich students: Use ASSIST: Assignment Submission and Tracking System
Step One: Print coversheet
Go to www.assist.uqi.uq.edu.au > Generate coversheet.
Step Two: Submit assignment
Scan the barcode on your student card OR enter your student number via the touch-screen keypad and press OK.
Scan the barcode on your assignment cover sheet.
Wait for submission confirmation on screen, and place your assignment into the numbered box as prompted on the screen.
The system will automatically reset for the next submission.
Note: that you must hand your assignment in at the campus at which you are enrolled.

SPECIAL EXAMS

Midsemester Exams
Midsemester exams are the responsibility of the Course Coordinator. The Faculty has no jurisdiction over midsemester examinations, hence all correspondence and documentation needs to be forwarded directly to the Course Coordinator. A student who fails to sit the midsemester exam at the scheduled time will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the Course Coordinator is supplied with documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence either prior to, or no later than two working days after, the midsemester examination. In such cases, the Course Coordinator has three options - (1) award a mark of zero if the evidence is judged to be insufficient or unsatisfactory; (2) hold an oral special examination; or (3) reweight other assessment items such as the final examination. There are no written special mid-semester examinations in the UQ Business School. The choice as to whether there is an oral special midsemester examination or a reweighting of assessment is at the discretion of the course coordinator and not the individual student involved.

Final Exams
A student who is unable to sit or complete a scheduled final examination must apply for a special examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held. Applications should be lodged at a Student Centre for submission to the Examinations Section, who will forward it to the appropriate Faculty/School administration. For evidence required and time limits see Assessment Rules 1A.14 as listed in the University of Queensland Handbook. Faculties may approve applications for special examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Special examinations are not automatically granted. All applications are assessed on a case-by-case basis and may require an interview where it is considered necessary to appraise the student's position in more detail. Directors of Studies will identify and provide appropriate advice to students applying repeatedly for special examinations, including referral to Student Support Services and the Disability Program.
